perturbation.original_text: Affiliates means subsidiaries or other entities that will be mutually agreed in writing.
perturbation.changed_text: Affiliates means any entity with which either party has a business relationship, irrespective of mutual agreement in writing. This includes, but is not limited to, suppliers, contractors, and potential future partners.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of 'Affiliates' is changed to include any entity with a business relationship, removing the requirement for mutual written agreement. This creates an in-text contradiction with other clauses that assume a more restricted definition of 'Affiliates' as entities that have been mutually agreed upon in writing. This may affect obligations and rights assigned to Affiliates within the contract, thus making it unclear to determine with which party to engage.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each Party shall, during the course of its normal business, use reasonable endeavours subject to the terms of this Agreement to: 4.2.1 promote and market the experience and capabilities of the Parties in order to identify opportunities for W2E, such promotion and marketing activities to be as agreed by the Parties from time to time;
perturbation.justification: The original definition of 'Affiliates' required mutual written agreement, ensuring a limited and well-defined scope. The changed text broadens this definition to include any entity with a business relationship, removing the mutual agreement requirement. This contradicts the contradicted text ('Each Party shall...promote and market...as agreed by the Parties from time to time') because the marketing activities were originally intended to be agreed upon only with mutually agreed-upon affiliates. Now, the broader definition of 'Affiliates' could include entities with which there hasn't been mutual agreement, leading to uncertainty about the scope of marketing and promotional activities, thus resulting in an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each party may terminate this Agreement if in the 12 months period immediately prior to such termination they fail to achieve the target annual business levels set out in Schedule 2 of the Manufacturing Agreement between the Parties.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party may terminate this Agreement if they 'believe' in their own judgment that in the 12 months period immediately prior to such termination they fail to achieve the target annual business levels set out in Schedule 2 of the Manufacturing Agreement between the Parties.
perturbation.explanation: Adding the words 'believe' and 'in their own judgment' means that the term has been changed and made vague by letting each party decide if they can terminate the agreement based on their beliefs. This directly contradicts other termination clauses in the text that specify the material and persistent breach of the agreement as a ground to terminate. By adding 'believe', this enables subjective interpretation, thus potentially making it difficult to enforce.
perturbation.contradicted_text: the other Party commits a material or persistent breach of the provisions of this Agreement (a "Breach"), provided that if the Breach is remediable, the other Party shall be given thirty (30) days to remedy the Breach from receipt of the first Party's written notice specifying the nature of the Breach and requesting that the same be remedied.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a subjective element ('believe' and 'in their own judgment') for terminating the agreement based on achieving target annual business levels. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which specifies 'a material or persistent breach of the provisions of this Agreement' as a ground for termination, subject to a notice and cure period. The original text provided an objective standard, whereas the changed text allows for termination based on subjective belief, creating inconsistency and potential unenforceability. This aligns with the definition of 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' as 'different sections of a contract provide conflicting... obligations'. The 'believe' standard weakens the need for an actual breach and directly contradicts the 'Breach' requirement in the contradicted text.
